title: Control plane anatomy
summary: Four core components run the cluster: API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager. Know which does what.
points: **kube-apiserver** — the only component that talks to etcd. Every read and write goes through it. The hub of the cluster.
**etcd** — distributed, consistent key-value store. Holds ALL cluster state. Lose etcd = lose the cluster.
**kube-scheduler** — watches for pods with no node assigned, picks one based on resources, affinity, taints. It does not run containers.
**kube-controller-manager** — runs controllers (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Service, etc.) that drive actual state toward desired state.
**cloud-controller-manager** — same idea but for cloud-provider-specific things (load balancers, persistent disks, node lifecycle).
traps: Don't confuse etcd with a cache — it's the source of truth.
kubelet and kube-proxy run on every node, not on the control plane (on managed nodes anyway).

title: Workload controllers: pick the right one
summary: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — each solves a different shape of workload. The exam trades on picking the right one.
points: **Deployment** — stateless, interchangeable replicas. Web apps, APIs. Rolling updates for free.
**StatefulSet** — stable network identity, ordered startup, stable storage. Databases, Kafka, anything with identity.
**DaemonSet** — one pod per node (or per matching node). Log shippers, CNI agents, node exporters.
**Job** — run to completion. Batch processing, migrations.
**CronJob** — Jobs on a schedule. Periodic cleanup, reports.
traps: 'Runs on every node' is ALWAYS a DaemonSet hint, never a Deployment.
StatefulSet pods get predictable names (app-0, app-1); Deployment pods get random hashes.
Jobs are NOT the same as CronJobs — a plain Job runs once.

title: Service types: ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer vs ExternalName
summary: Four Service types, each with different exposure. The exam loves to ask 'which type would you use for X?'.
points: **ClusterIP** (default) — stable virtual IP reachable only inside the cluster. Pod-to-pod communication.
**NodePort** — opens the same port (30000–32767) on every node. External clients hit any node's IP:port.
**LoadBalancer** — asks the cloud provider for an external LB, which routes to NodePort behind the scenes.
**ExternalName** — pure DNS alias to an external hostname (CNAME). No proxying, no selectors.
traps: NodePort is not 'node-level' — it's just a port opened on every node. The target is still pods chosen by the selector.
LoadBalancer requires cloud integration. On bare metal, it stays pending unless you have MetalLB or similar.
ExternalName has no ClusterIP, no endpoints, and no selector.

title: Probes: liveness, readiness, startup
summary: Three probes with different jobs. Mixing them up is a classic exam trap.
points: **Liveness** — is the container healthy? If it fails, kubelet restarts the container.
**Readiness** — is the container ready to serve? If it fails, the pod is removed from Service endpoints (but NOT restarted).
**Startup** — gate for liveness/readiness during slow boots. Once it succeeds, the other probes take over.
traps: Readiness failure does NOT restart. It just pulls you out of the load balancer.
Startup probe protects slow-starting apps from being killed by an aggressive liveness check.
A pod with no readiness probe is 'ready' the moment its container starts — often too eager.

title: ConfigMaps and Secrets
summary: Both inject configuration into pods. Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default.
points: Both can be consumed as env vars OR mounted as files.
Mounted files update when the ConfigMap/Secret changes; env vars do NOT update until the pod restarts.
Secrets are base64-encoded for transport, NOT encrypted. Enable encryption at rest in etcd if you need real protection.
Size limit is 1 MiB for both.
traps: 'Secrets are encrypted' is wrong by default. Base64 is encoding, not encryption.
Environment variables from a ConfigMap do not hot-reload on change.

title: Pod networking: every pod gets an IP
summary: Kubernetes networking model: flat network, every pod has its own IP, all pods can reach all pods without NAT.
points: Each pod has a unique IP, routable inside the cluster.
Containers in the same pod share the network namespace — they reach each other on localhost.
The **pause container** holds the network namespace so app containers can come and go without losing the IP.
CNI plugins (Calico, Flannel, Cilium, Weave) implement the actual networking.
traps: Two containers in one pod cannot listen on the same port (they share the namespace).
'Containers in a pod share an IP' is true; 'pods share an IP' is false.

title: The autoscaler trio: HPA vs VPA vs CA
summary: Three different autoscalers, three different axes. Don't confuse them.
points: **Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)** — scales pod replica count horizontally based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.
**Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)** — adjusts pod resource requests/limits vertically. Typically recreates the pod to apply.
**Cluster Autoscaler (CA)** — adds or removes nodes based on pending pods that can't be scheduled.
traps: HPA and VPA together can conflict on CPU metrics — don't let both target the same resource.
Cluster Autoscaler works WITH HPA: HPA schedules more pods, pending ones trigger CA to add nodes.

title: NetworkPolicy: resource vs enforcement
summary: NetworkPolicy is a Kubernetes API object, but the CNI plugin actually enforces it. If your CNI doesn't support it, the policy is silently ignored.
points: Pods with no NetworkPolicy = allow all in, allow all out.
Apply a NetworkPolicy selecting a pod → that pod becomes 'isolated' for the specified direction (ingress/egress).
Rules are additive — multiple policies OR together.
Enforced by the CNI. Calico and Cilium enforce. Flannel (by itself) does NOT.
traps: Applying a NetworkPolicy with Flannel and expecting enforcement → silent failure.
NetworkPolicy is namespace-scoped unless you use a Cilium CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy or similar.

title: Storage: PV, PVC, StorageClass
summary: Storage abstraction in three layers. Admins provision PVs (or a StorageClass); users claim them with PVCs.
points: **PersistentVolume (PV)** — cluster resource representing actual storage (EBS volume, NFS share, etc.).
**PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)** — a request for storage. Bound to a PV that matches size, access mode, class.
**StorageClass** — blueprint for dynamic provisioning. PVC referencing a class → PV created on demand.
**Access modes** — ReadWriteOnce (one node), ReadOnlyMany, ReadWriteMany, ReadWriteOncePod.
traps: ReadWriteOnce = one node (not one pod). Multiple pods on same node can share.
A PVC without a StorageClass matches only pre-created PVs (static provisioning).

title: Ingress and Gateway API
summary: Ingress is the original HTTP router for Kubernetes. Gateway API is the successor — more expressive, role-oriented.
points: **Ingress** — L7 routing rules (host/path → service). Needs an Ingress controller (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy, etc.) to actually do anything.
**Gateway API** — newer standard. Separates concerns across roles: cluster operator (GatewayClass), infra provider (Gateway), app dev (HTTPRoute).
Ingress is widely deployed; Gateway API is becoming the recommended path forward.
Neither provisions a load balancer on its own — the controller does.
traps: An Ingress resource with no controller installed does nothing. Creating the resource is not enough.
Don't mix up Ingress (L7, HTTP) with Service of type LoadBalancer (L4, any TCP/UDP).

title: Cloud native principles: 12-factor and immutability
summary: The mental model behind the whole CNCF ecosystem. Stateless, declarative, immutable.
points: **Immutable infrastructure** — don't patch servers in place; rebuild and replace. Containers embody this.
**Declarative** — describe desired state (YAML), let controllers drive actual state toward it.
**Stateless apps** — state lives in backing services (DBs, object stores), not on the compute node.
**12-factor principles** — config in env, logs as event streams, disposability, dev/prod parity.
traps: 'Cloud native' doesn't mean 'in the cloud'. It means built using cloud-native patterns — can run on-prem.
Containers ≠ cloud native by themselves. Running a pet container is not cloud native.

title: Service mesh: what it gives you
summary: A mesh layers observability, mTLS, and traffic control on top of service-to-service calls — usually via sidecar proxies.
points: Common features: mTLS between services, retries, circuit breaking, request-level metrics, traffic splitting.
Data plane = proxies (Envoy, linkerd2-proxy) next to each pod. Control plane = configures them.
**Istio** — powerful, complex, Envoy-based.
**Linkerd** — lightweight, ultralight Rust proxy.
**Consul Connect**, **Cilium Service Mesh** — alternatives.
traps: A mesh is not a load balancer replacement — it runs alongside existing Service discovery.
Sidecar-per-pod meshes add resource cost; sidecarless meshes (Ambient, Cilium) trade different tradeoffs.

title: CNCF landscape: who does what
summary: The 'which project lives in which category' grid is where most people lose points. Memorize the categories.
points: **Orchestration & scheduling** — Kubernetes (the only one that matters for KCNA).
**Container runtime** — containerd, CRI-O, Docker (deprecated as a runtime in k8s).
**Networking (CNI)** — Calico, Cilium, Flannel, Weave.
**Storage (CSI)** — Rook, Longhorn.
**Service mesh** — Istio, Linkerd.
**Observability** — Prometheus (metrics), Jaeger (tracing), Fluentd (logs), OpenTelemetry (all three).
**App delivery** — ArgoCD, Flux (GitOps); Helm (packaging).
**Service proxy** — Envoy.
**Registries** — Harbor.
traps: Prometheus is metrics ONLY. Not logs, not traces.
OpenTelemetry is a signal pipeline, not a backend — it feeds Prometheus/Jaeger/etc.
Helm is packaging, not GitOps.

title: Observability: three pillars, three tools
summary: Metrics, logs, traces. Know the canonical CNCF project for each.
points: **Metrics** — aggregated numbers over time. Prometheus. Scrapes `/metrics` endpoints.
**Logs** — discrete events with context. Fluentd, Fluent Bit, Loki.
**Traces** — request path across services. Jaeger, Zipkin.
**OpenTelemetry** — unified instrumentation for all three signals. Replaces OpenTracing + OpenCensus.
traps: Prometheus ≠ logs. A classic exam trap.
Grafana is a visualization layer, not a storage backend. It reads from Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, etc.

title: GitOps: pull vs push
summary: Git as the source of truth for desired cluster state. Controllers reconcile actual state to match.
points: **Pull model** — agent in cluster watches the repo and pulls changes (ArgoCD, Flux). Safer — the cluster never opens an inbound hole.
**Push model** — CI pipeline runs `kubectl apply` against the cluster. Simpler, but CI needs cluster creds.
**ArgoCD** — UI-heavy, CRD-based. Strong audit trail.
**Flux** — more unixy, split into smaller controllers. Lighter footprint.
**Helm and Kustomize** both compose cleanly with GitOps tools.
traps: GitOps = Git is source of truth, not 'we use Git for our YAML'. The reconciliation loop is what makes it GitOps.
CI/CD tools (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) are not GitOps tools on their own.

title: Deployment strategies: rolling, blue-green, canary
summary: Three strategies for shipping a new version with different tradeoffs.
points: **Rolling update** (k8s default for Deployment) — replace pods gradually, controlled by maxSurge/maxUnavailable. No extra infra.
**Blue-green** — two full environments. Cut over traffic at once. Instant rollback, double the infra.
**Canary** — send a small % of traffic to the new version first. Validate, then ramp. Needs traffic-splitting (service mesh or Ingress features).
traps: Rolling updates give you progressive rollout but no traffic-weight control — it's all-or-nothing per pod.
Canary needs an intelligent router; NodePort + plain Service can't do weighted splits.
